QF.id: XIII Introduction to the Underlying Markets_19

question.text: In bond markets, what is the relationship between bond prices and interest rates?

answer.text: Bond prices and interest rates move inversely — when interest rates rise, bond prices fall, and vice versa
Bond prices and interest rates move in the same direction — both rise or fall together
Bond prices are unaffected by changes in interest rates
The relationship depends on whether the bond is government or corporate

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Bond prices and yields have an inverse relationship. When market interest rates rise, existing bonds with lower coupons become less attractive, so their prices fall. Conversely, when rates fall, existing bonds with higher coupons become more valuable. **The Trap:** This inverse relationship is universal for fixed-rate bonds regardless of issuer. Some candidates confuse price with yield — the yield moves with market rates, but the price moves inversely. **Cross-Exam Context:** Series IV Ch1 covers bond price-yield relationship extensively including duration and convexity. Series I covers how interest rate differentials affect currency pricing. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** This inverse relationship is the foundation of interest rate risk — IR derivative strategies (hedging with futures, options) are designed to manage this price risk.
